channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Brookings Institution Report: Over 100,000 Family Separations in Trump Crackdown
item.description: The Brookings Institution suggests that federal statistics are an undercount because immigrant parents are not being asked about or not disclosing their American children.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan and Jeff Adelson
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Children have been separated from their parents, according to a new report, as the Trump administration continues its deportation push.

item.title: How a Funding Pause Derailed an Artificial Heart for Babies
item.description: James Antaki’s efforts to develop a baby’s heart were close to success when his federal funding was cut off. The grants were eventually restored; rebuilding what was lost wasn’t so easy.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj
item.media:credit: Celia Talbot Tobin for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Antaki, a biomedical engineer at Cornell. He had been developing an artificial heart for young children. Roughly 14,000 children and babies are hospitalized with heart failure each year in the United States.

item.title: Abortion Pill Lawsuit Leaves Trump Silent, and in a Political Bind
item.description: Louisiana wants the Food and Drug Administration to curtail access to the medication. Doing so could cost Republicans at the polls.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:description: A lawsuit against the F.D.A. threatens access to medication abortion, and the Trump administration has remained strikingly silent about the case, even as it reached the Supreme Court.

item.title: Trump’s Approval Sinks Amid Unpopular War, Darkening G.O.P. Prospects
item.description: With the midterms nearing, President Trump’s approval rating has hit a second-term low as voters question his handling of the economy, according to the latest New York Times/Siena poll.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer, Ruth Igielnik and Camille Baker
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Keisha Lance Bottoms Was Atlanta’s Mayor in the Pandemic. Can She Win the Governor’s Race?
item.description: Keisha Lance Bottoms is the Democrats’ front-runner as the Georgia primary for governor approaches. But her party rivals are trying to ensure she has to face a runoff.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas and Michelle Baruchman
item.media:description: Keisha Lance Bottoms arriving this month to vote early in the Georgia primary.

item.title: Why Did Some LIRR Unions Decide to Strike?
item.description: Members of the five unions that are on strike made more than $136,000 last year on average, according to M.T.A. figures. But they have not received a raise since 2022.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The five negotiating unions represent about half of the Long Island Rail Road’s workers.

item.title: Tonys 2026 Predictions: Who Will Win? And Who Should?
item.description: Our chief theater critic looks at this year’s nominees and makes some predictions (and recommendations).
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)
item.media:description: Susannah Flood, seated, and fellow cast members in “Liberation.”

item.title: How the Music of ‘Schmigadoon!’ Echoes Broadway’s Past
item.description: The composer Cinco Paul discusses the clever references to classic musicals everywhere you look (and hear) in his new Broadway show.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.media:description: “Schmigadoon!,” a musical satire adapted from the 2021 AppleTV+ series, is now playing at the Nederlander Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Indictment and Impeachment Only Made Him Stronger. Remind You of Anyone?
item.description: The fate of Ken Paxton, the conservative Texas firebrand, could decide whether Republicans keep control of the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kruse and Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times

item.title: In Closed-Door Talks, U.S. Demands a Major Role in Greenland
item.description: Greenlandic officials worry about the direction of the negotiations aimed at defusing President Trump’s threats to seize their island. But they have little leverage.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman, Maya Tekeli, Anton Troianovski and Eric Schmitt
item.media:credit: Ivor Prickett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ilulisaat, Greenland, a town where a Chinese state company nearly won a contract to build an airport in 2018. After U.S. officials pressured Denmark to step in, Greenland opted for a Danish company.

item.title: Trump’s Taiwan Gambit is Already a Gift to China
item.description: President Trump’s open willingness to hold up a $14 billion Taiwan arms package is a win for Beijing. Now China could be weighing how to keep the weapons on ice for as long as it can.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley and Amy Chang Chien
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump with the Chinese leader, Xi Jinping, in Beijing, on Thursday.

item.title: Catastrophe Is Emerging in the World’s Most Vulnerable Places
item.description: The humanitarian relief system, decimated by cuts, faces a grave challenge as the Middle East war causes soaring costs for food, fuel and fertilizer.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman and Finbarr O’Reilly
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Muslima Ibrahim Mohamed, 38, holds her 2-day-old son, Noor Mohamed.

item.title: Actually, Democracy Dies in H.R.
item.description: New research sheds light on how mediocre employees help would-be authoritarians maintain power.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Taub
item.category: Making a Career in Dictatorship: The Secret Logic behind Repression and Coups (Book)
item.media:description: Argentine security forces seizing a young man in 1982 in Buenos Aires during the Dirty War.

item.title: Trump Warns Iran the ‘Clock Is Ticking’ as Tensions Surge Again in Middle East
item.description: The president has sought to force Iran to accept his terms on its nuclear program or else face renewed war. An emboldened Iran has rebuffed Trump’s demands.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump said in a social media post that Iran had to move fast “or there won’t be anything left,” adding “the Clock is Ticking.”

item.title: Blocked Strait of Hormuz Leads to New Opportunities for Syria
item.description: The latest war in the Middle East has created new economic opportunities for Syria thanks to its geography.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Iraqi trucks carrying oil along the highway near the Baniyas port and refinery in Syria in April.

item.title: Gun Found in Mangione’s Backpack Can be Used as Evidence, Judge Says
item.description: The ruling was a partial victory for prosecutors in the murder trial of Luigi Mangione. A state judge ruled that some other material and statements could not be used.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko and Anusha Bayya
item.media:description: Luigi Mangione’s lawyers have argued that the evidence found in his backpack when he was arrested was obtained illegally and cannot be used at trial.

item.title: What to Know About the Latest Ebola Outbreak
item.description: The World Health Organization declared a global health emergency after about 80 people died from the virus in the Democratic Republic of Congo.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: A motorcycle taxi driver at a hospital in Goma, Democratic Republic of Congo, on Sunday.

item.title: Americans Are Among Those Affected by Ebola Outbreak, C.D.C. Says
item.description: A small number of U.S. citizens have been directly affected by the outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda, the C.D.C. said on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: People wait near an ambulance at a hospital in Bunia, Congo on Sunday.

item.title: He Shut Liquor Stores and Banned Abortion, All for the Glory of Russia
item.description: A firebrand governor aims to transform his region into a laboratory for the Kremlin’s reactionary ideals.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko and Mary Gelman
item.media:description: A mural reading “the powerhouse of the Russian world” in the city of Vologda, Russia, in February.

item.title: Russia’s New Human Rights Commissioner Accused of Helping Kidnap Ukrainian Children
item.description: Yana Lantratova was instrumental in helping the chairman of her party to illegally adopt an infant girl from Russian-occupied Ukraine, the Ukrainian authorities say.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Yana Lantratova, in Moscow in October, will report directly to President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia.

item.title: Mamdani’s First City-Owned Grocery Store Is Planned for the South Bronx
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani said that the store will open in the Hunts Point neighborhood next year. Some merchants are pushing back.
item.dc:creator: Emma G. Fitzsimmons
item.media:credit: Elias Williams for The New York Times
item.media:description: A core campaign promise of Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who unveiled the plan for Hunts Point in a news conference on Monday, involved opening a city-run grocery store in each borough.

item.title: Trump Has Done More Than Misjudge Iran
item.description: The war has revealed the true puniness of Trump’s power
item.dc:creator: Lydia Polgreen
item.media:credit: Max Slobodda for The New York Times

item.title: The Fighting Never Ends
item.description: We look at what happens when countries say they’re going to stop shooting at each other.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The site of an Israeli airstrike in Beirut’s southern suburbs this month.

item.title: Federal Prosecutors Move to Drop Bribery Case Against Indian Magnate
item.description: Gautam Adani had been accused of lying to American investors about payoffs in India. The end of the case came as President Trump has signaled a transactional approach to justice.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar and Alex Travelli
item.media:description: Gautam Adani’s companies have powered India’s rise as a global economic power.

item.title: Two Military Jets Collide in Midair at Idaho Air Show
item.description: Video shared on social media showed the jets striking each other and spinning toward the ground. Four crew members safely ejected before the planes crashed, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz and Edgar Sandoval
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: U.S. Air Force photo by Senior Airman Xavier Wilson
item.media:description: The U.S. Air Force demonstration squadron Thunderbirds at the Gunfighter Skies air show at Mountain Home Air Force Base in Idaho on Sunday. Two EA-18G Growler jets that were performing in a demonstration collided.

item.title: Millions Recovered in FIFA Corruption Scandal May Now Be Missing Again Before World Cup
item.description: Ahead of the World Cup, the head of South American soccer faces an ethics complaint he received payments recovered from a 2015 investigation that shook global soccer.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: Confederation of North, Central American and Caribbean Association Football (CONCACAF)
item.media:description: Alejandro Dominguez, the president of Conmebol, the South American soccer federation, in Quito, Ecuador, last year.

item.title: Children’s Mental Health Visits Have Shot Up, Research Shows
item.description: Doctor’s visits for children’s anxiety rose by more than 250 percent over 10 years, according to a study of nearly two million children.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:credit: Annie Flanagan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Visits were counted as mental health visits if a diagnostic code was included in the claim, either because the child or the family raised the issue or because the child screened positive for mental health symptoms during the visit.
